reply:
[[BLOCK:srt_158]]
Speaker 3: In your view, what unique needs or strengths do Chinese students bring to this field?

[[BLOCK:srt_159]]
Speaker 1: Let me start by saying that Golden Gate University has always placed a strong emphasis on the Chinese market.

[[BLOCK:srt_160]]
Speaker 1: As early as 40 years ago, Golden Gate University was already offering degree programs in China—for example, delivering academic programs to employees at Bank of America’s branches there.

[[BLOCK:srt_161]]
Speaker 1: We have consistently viewed China as a key growth market and hope to serve more students here by expanding access to our degree programs.

[[BLOCK:srt_162]]
Speaker 1: We previously launched our Master of Science in Artificial Intelligence program in San Francisco, and we would very much like to introduce it to China as well, giving Chinese students the opportunity to enroll.

[[BLOCK:srt_163]]
Speaker 1: Chinese students typically have a strong academic foundation, extensive hands-on experience, and solid technical skills. These are all clear strengths.

[[BLOCK:srt_164]]
Speaker 1: In both artificial intelligence research and interdisciplinary applications, China and the United States are global leaders.

[[BLOCK:srt_165]]
Speaker 1: So whether in AI research or education, China’s role simply cannot be overlooked.

[[BLOCK:srt_166]]
Speaker 1: Globally, the two countries advancing most rapidly in artificial intelligence are China and the United States.

[[BLOCK:srt_167]]
Speaker 1: I prefer to see that as a shared future.

[[BLOCK:srt_168]]
Speaker 1: Many people talk about competition between China and the United States in artificial intelligence, but from my perspective, what matters more going forward is integration and collaboration. The future of the world is closely tied to artificial intelligence, and only by working together can China and the United States help safeguard that future.

[[BLOCK:srt_169]]
Speaker 2: There’s another point to consider. From San Francisco’s perspective, we are located on the Pacific Rim. In terms of demographics, trade and economic ties, as well as cultural and social exchange, China is closely connected to us—so much so that in some ways, it feels almost like a neighbor.
